turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been getting this tightness in my chest lately, like a pressure sensation under my breastbone. It's been going on for about three days now, and it's starting to worry me.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's hard to explain, but it feels like someone is squeezing my chest from the inside. It comes and goes, but when it's there, it's pretty constant. I get a little winded just walking up the stairs at home, which isn't normal for me, so that's been adding to my anxiety.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: Yes, when I'm experiencing the chest tightness, I also start sweating a lot. It's like I'm pouring sweat from my head down to my toes, and it doesn't stop until the pressure eases up.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: Yes, definitely. The sweating is one of the most noticeable things for me during those episodes - I'm talking profuse sweating all over my body, not just on my chest or upper back like you'd expect with a cold sweat. It's really intense and doesn't seem to stop until the tightness in my chest subsides.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: Actually, yes - profuse sweating does occur during the episodes of chest tightness. I'd say it's one of the first things that happens when I start feeling that pressure in my chest, and it lasts for as long as the tightness is there.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The discomfort feels like it's centered under my breastbone, where I mentioned earlier. It's always been pretty consistent in that one area, although when I'm really exerting myself and feeling short of breath, the tightness can kind of radiate outward a bit towards my shoulders or upper back.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: Yes, during the more severe episodes, I do get tingling sensations in my left arm. It feels kind of numb and tingly at the same time, and it usually happens when the chest tightness is really bad.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: Yes, during the more severe episodes, I do get tingling sensations in my left arm. It feels kind of numb and tingly at the same time, and it usually happens when the chest tightness is really bad.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: Only in my left arm, during the more severe episodes of chest tightness. It feels like a numb and tingly sensation all at once.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: Yes, I have noticed some mild bilateral ankle swelling lately. My shoes feel tighter than usual, especially in the mornings when my ankles are a bit puffy. It's not too severe, but it's definitely noticeable and has been happening for about a week now.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: Yes, my shoes do feel tighter than usual, especially on my ankles. It's like they're swelling up a bit more than normal, and it's been going on for about a week now. The puffiness is pretty mild, but it's definitely noticeable in the mornings when I get out of bed.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: Yes, during the chest tightness episodes, I do experience a mild nausea. It's not severe enough to make me vomit, but it's definitely there, and it usually happens at the same time as the sweating and tingling sensations in my arm.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yes, I experience a mild nausea during those episodes. It's not severe enough to make me vomit, but it's definitely there, and it usually happens at the same time as the sweating and tingling sensations in my arm.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: No, I haven't experienced any fever or chills. The sweating I mentioned earlier is more like a profuse, intense sweat when I'm experiencing the chest tightness episodes - it's not really related to temperature regulation. And no, I haven't had any night sweats outside of those episodes either.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: Actually, my diet has been a bit concerning me lately too - I've been eating a lot of fast food, about 5-6 days out of the week. It's not something I'm proud of, but it's just been my go-to because of my busy schedule as a truck driver.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: To be honest, my lifestyle hasn't been great lately. I've been driving a lot for work and it's hard to find time to exercise or eat healthy. As you mentioned earlier, I've been eating a lot of fast food - about 5-6 days out of the week. I try to tell myself it's not that big of a deal, but deep down I know it's not ideal.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: To be honest, I'm pretty sedentary. As a truck driver, I spend most of my time on the road and don't get much opportunity to exercise. I try to stay active when I'm at home with my wife, but it's not something I prioritize. To be frank, I'd say I'm probably inactive 90% of the time.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, it's pretty standard - fast food, 5-6 days out of the week. I mean, when I'm on the road, it's just easier to grab something quick at a truck stop or a restaurant near my route. It's not like I'm eating salads and fruits every day, no... it's more like burgers, fries, sandwiches... you know, typical fast food fare. When I'm home with my wife, we might try to cook healthier meals, but on the road, it's just convenience over nutrition, unfortunately.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My mom has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. She's still alive at 78, which I guess is a good thing.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My mom has hypertension and type 2 diabetes, like I mentioned before. She's been dealing with those conditions for years, but she's still doing okay at 78.
